about.heroTitle: WHERE ANCIENT EGYPT MEETS THE FUTURE
about.heroDesc: The Grand Egyptian Museum is a landmark destination dedicated to preserving, presenting, and celebrating the remarkable heritage of ancient Egyptian civilization. Located near the Great Pyramid of Khufu at Giza, the museum stands as one of the world's most significant cultural projects.
about.storyTitle: A NEW HOME FOR EGYPT'S TIMELESS LEGACY
about.storyText1: Created to share the heritage of ancient Egypt through a world-class cultural experience, the Grand Egyptian Museum invites visitors to encounter thousands of years of history, artistry, belief, and innovation in one extraordinary destination.
about.storyText2: The GEM complex covers about 500,000 square meters, making it one of the largest museum complexes in the world. Its main building includes a 92,000-square-meter museum section and a 40,000-square-meter commercial section.
about.fact1Value: Near the Great Pyramid of Khufu, Giza
about.fact3Value: From the Prehistoric Period to the Roman Period
about.fact4Value: One of the largest archaeological museums dedicated to a single civilization
about.missionTitle: PRESERVING THE PAST, INSPIRING THE FUTURE
about.missionDesc: The mission of the Grand Egyptian Museum is to share the remarkable heritage of ancient Egyptian civilization through preservation, education, research, and meaningful cultural experiences.
about.val1Desc: We are committed to safeguarding Egypt's unique cultural heritage through advanced conservation, restoration, and responsible stewardship.
about.val2Desc: We believe museums are places of discovery. Through exhibitions, educational initiatives, and cultural programming, the GEM creates opportunities for curiosity and learning.
about.val3Desc: The GEM is committed to environmental, social, and financial sustainability, aligning its efforts with Egypt's Vision 2030.
about.val4Desc: The museum seeks to create an inclusive cultural environment that welcomes diverse audiences through accessible experiences and cultural exchange.
about.archTitle: INSPIRED BY THE PYRAMIDS, DESIGNED FOR THE FUTURE
about.archDesc1: The design reflects a dialogue between heritage and innovation. Chosen through an international competition of 1,557 firms from 83 countries, the concept presents the museum inspired by rays from the three pyramids.
about.archDesc2: Blending modern design with ancient Egyptian architecture, the building incorporates local materials, natural ventilation, and environmentally conscious systems.
about.timelineSub: Discover the major milestones in the journey of the Grand Egyptian Museum, from its initial concept to its opening
about.expDesc: The Grand Egyptian Museum offers more than gallery spaces. Visitors can explore a wider cultural experience through exhibitions, the Children's Museum, the Arts and Crafts Centre, educational initiatives, and public programming that connect heritage with creativity, learning, and contemporary life.
about.card1Desc: Interactive learning experiences for young visitors
about.card2Desc: Hands-on cultural workshops and creative activities
about.card3Desc: Workshops, lectures, and guided learning experiences
about.collDesc: From iconic artefacts to curated galleries, the museum's collection reveals the cultural achievements of ancient Egypt across different eras. Each object offers a unique window into the beliefs, daily life, artistry, and imagination of a civilization that continues to inspire the world.
about.bigCard1Desc: A dedicated section in the Grand Egyptian Museum displaying over 5,000 treasures of Tutankhamun, telling the story of his life, burial, and journey to the afterlife.
about.bigCard2Desc: Journey through the major periods of ancient Egyptian civilization in galleries designed to reveal continuity, change, and cultural brilliance across time.
about.bigCard3Desc: A rich display in the Grand Egyptian Museum featuring statues, tools, and everyday objects that reveal the art, culture, and daily life of ancient Egypt.
about.sustTitle: A MUSEUM WITH A RESPONSIBLE VISION
about.sustDesc1: The Grand Egyptian Museum is committed to being a global leader in sustainability by integrating scientific practices into its design and operations. Its efforts are guided by three main goals: environmental sustainability, social sustainability, and financial sustainability.
about.sustDesc2: The museum has also been recognized for its green building approach and sustainability standards, including EDGE ADVANCE certification, making it the first green museum in Africa and the Middle East.
about.ctaTitle: DISCOVER THE LEGACY OF ANCIENT EGYPT
about.ctaDesc: Explore one of the world's most ambitious cultural destinations and experience the story of ancient Egypt through extraordinary collections, immersive spaces, and a museum designed to inspire wonder.

explore.heroSub: Walk through 5,000 years of civilization — arranged by era and theme

explore.hall12Desc: Markets, crafts, and families under foreign rule.
explore.hall07Desc: Scribes, soldiers, artisans at the empire's peak.
explore.hall08Desc: Thutmose, Ramses, and the conquering kings.
explore.hall09Desc: Karnak, Luxor, and the solar theology.
explore.hall06Desc: Coffin texts and the democratization of the hereafter.
explore.hall05Desc: The divine authority of Mentuhotep's line.

childs.title: GEM Children's Museum
childs.desc1: Calling all young explorers for an exciting ancient Egyptian adventure at the GEM Children's Museum.
childs.desc2: This dedicated space is designed to spark curiosity and imagination through hands-on learning experiences tailored specifically for young minds. Children can discover the wonders of ancient Egyptian civilisation in a fun, engaging, and interactive environment.
childs.info1: Hands-on activities designed for children aged 6–12
childs.info2: Interactive zones introducing ancient Egypt through play
childs.info3: An interactive guidebook is available to enhance the experience
childs.info4: Children must be accompanied by an adult at all times
childs.why1: Fun and educational experience for kids

discovery.title: GEM Discovery Challenge
discovery.desc1: Make your visit to the Grand Egyptian Museum unforgettable by joining the GEM Discovery Challenge with your friends and family.
discovery.desc2: Explore a selection of fascinating artifacts through a fun and interactive experience. Follow clues, solve puzzles, and uncover the hidden treasure key. Winners of the challenge will receive a special souvenir to remember their adventure.
discovery.schedule1: The game is offered daily at 12 PM, 2 PM, 4 PM
discovery.info1: Each participant must have their own ticket
discovery.info2: The challenge can take up to 1 hour
discovery.info4: Children under 12 must be accompanied by someone aged 16+ or an adult
discovery.info5: A smartphone is required to participate
discovery.why3: Discover ancient Egyptian artifacts in an exciting way

tutMask.pageTitle: Golden Mask of Tutankhamun — Grand Egyptian Museum
tutMask.subtitle: The burial mask that became the face of ancient Egypt
tutMask.lead: This mask of solid gold is one of the most famous objects in the world — the death mask of the boy king Tutankhamun, who ruled Egypt over 3,300 years ago.
tutMask.body1: Crafted from two layers of high-karat gold and inlaid with lapis lazuli, carnelian, obsidian, and coloured glass, the mask was placed directly over the face of the king's mummified body. Its weight of more than 10 kilograms of gold speaks to the wealth of the New Kingdom at the height of its power.
tutMask.body2: The headdress is the striped nemes worn only by pharaohs, flanked by a vulture goddess Nekhbet and a cobra goddess Wadjet — the protective deities of Upper and Lower Egypt. The false beard, braided and curled, symbolizes the king's divine status, while the broad collar is composed of twelve rows of semi-precious stones.
tutMask.body3: Discovered by British archaeologist Howard Carter in 1922 inside the nearly untouched tomb KV62 in the Valley of the Kings, the mask has since become the most recognizable artifact from ancient Egypt — a symbol of pharaonic power, craftsmanship, and the timeless mystery of Tutankhamun himself.
tutMask.cite: — Howard Carter, on entering the tomb, 1922

throne.pageTitle: Golden Throne of Tutankhamun — Grand Egyptian Museum
throne.subtitle: The ceremonial throne that captured the king's intimate royal life
throne.lead: The Golden Throne of Tutankhamun is one of the most exquisite pieces of furniture ever recovered from ancient Egypt — a ceremonial chair carved from wood and entirely covered in sheet gold and silver.
throne.body1: Inlaid with coloured glass, faience, and semi-precious stones, the throne is celebrated above all for the tender scene on its backrest: Queen Ankhesenamun anoints her young husband with perfumed oil, both bathed in the rays of the sun-disk Aten. It is a rare, intimate glimpse into royal life — an image of affection rather than authority.
throne.body2: The throne's arms are formed as winged uraei — protective cobra goddesses crowned with sun-disks — and its legs take the shape of lion's paws, symbolizing royal power and protection. Hieroglyphic inscriptions on the throne bear both of Tutankhamun's names, including his earlier name Tutankhaten, reflecting the religious transition of his reign.
throne.body3: Discovered in the Antechamber of tomb KV62 in 1922, Howard Carter described it as "the most beautiful thing that has yet been found in Egypt." Today, the throne stands as a masterpiece of New Kingdom craftsmanship and a window into the personal world of the boy king.
throne.quote: The most beautiful thing that has yet been found in Egypt.
throne.cite: — Howard Carter, on entering the tomb, 1922

tutHead.pageTitle: Head of Tutankhamun — Grand Egyptian Museum
tutHead.h1: Head of Tutankhamun emerging from a lotus
tutHead.subtitle: The young king reborn as the sun-god at the dawn of creation
tutHead.lead: Sculpted from wood and painted with remarkable delicacy, this small head shows the young Tutankhamun rising from the open petals of a blue lotus — a symbol of rebirth and the first moment of creation in Egyptian belief.
tutHead.body1: In the ancient Egyptian creation myth, the sun-god first emerged as a child from a lotus flower that rose out of the primeval waters of Nun. By depicting the king in this form, the sculpture identifies Tutankhamun with the sun at the instant of its daily rebirth — an act of divine renewal intended to accompany the king into the afterlife.
tutHead.body2: The features are rendered with striking naturalism: the elongated skull, full cheeks, and soft expression reflect the late Amarna artistic style inherited from the reign of his father, Akhenaten. The surface was carved from a single piece of wood, then coated with plaster and painted with fine detail.
tutHead.body3: Found in the corridor of tomb KV62 in 1922, the piece was among the first objects Howard Carter encountered upon entering the tomb. Today it is regarded as one of the most poetic and visually arresting works from the entire Tutankhamun collection — an intimate portrait that captures both the fragility and the divinity of the boy king.
tutHead.quote: The king rises like the sun from the eternal lotus.

ramses.pageTitle: Ramses II — The Great | Grand Egyptian Museum
ramses.subtitle: The warrior-king whose reign of 66 years became the golden age of imperial Egypt
ramses.lead: Ramses II — known to history as Ramses the Great — was the third pharaoh of the Nineteenth Dynasty, and one of the most celebrated rulers of ancient Egypt. His 66-year reign marked the zenith of the New Kingdom's power, wealth, and monumental ambition.
ramses.body1: He came to the throne in 1279 BC and ruled until his death at roughly 90 years old, outliving many of his own children. During his lifetime he led campaigns into Nubia, Libya, and the Levant, and fought the Hittite Empire at the legendary Battle of Kadesh — a confrontation that ended in the world's first known international peace treaty.
ramses.body2: Ramses transformed Egypt's landscape like no pharaoh before him. He built the rock-cut temples of Abu Simbel, expanded the great temple complex of Karnak, raised his mortuary temple the Ramesseum on the west bank of Thebes, and founded an entirely new capital in the Delta — Pi-Ramesses. Colossal statues of him still stand across Egypt, many usurped from earlier kings, others carved anew at unprecedented scale.
ramses.body3: His mummy was discovered in 1881 in the royal cache at Deir el-Bahari, hidden there in antiquity to protect it from tomb robbers. The Greeks knew him as Ozymandias — a name immortalized by the poet Shelley as a symbol of fallen empires and the passage of time.
ramses.quote: My name is Ozymandias, King of Kings; Look on my Works, ye Mighty, and despair!

king.pageTitle: Colossus of a Ptolemaic King — Grand Egyptian Museum
king.subtitle: Raised from the seabed after more than a thousand years beneath the Mediterranean
king.lead: Carved from red granite and standing five metres tall, this colossus depicts a Ptolemaic king in the traditional Egyptian style — a Greek-born ruler portrayed with the full iconography of a pharaoh, wearing the double crown of Upper and Lower Egypt and the royal shendyt kilt.
king.body1: The king stands with his left leg advanced, arms held firmly at his sides, his right fist gripping a small cylindrical object — a convention inherited from Old Kingdom royal statuary more than two millennia earlier. Though the inscriptions have not survived, the scale, style, and provenance of the statue point most strongly to Ptolemy II Philadelphus, who ruled Egypt between 285 and 246 BC.
king.body2: The colossus originally stood outside a temple of Amun-Gereb at Thonis-Heracleion, the legendary port city that was Egypt's main gateway to the Mediterranean for centuries. Then, around the 8th century AD, earthquakes and rising sea levels sent the entire city sinking beneath the waters of Abu Qir Bay off the coast of Alexandria — and the colossus went with it.
king.body3: In the year 2000, a joint mission led by the European Institute for Underwater Archaeology rediscovered the sunken city. Divers found the colossus lying on the seabed more than 6 kilometres offshore, together with its companion statue of a Ptolemaic queen — likely Arsinoe II, portrayed as the goddess Isis. The two statues toured Europe and the United States as part of the "Sunken Cities" exhibition before taking their permanent place in the atrium of the Grand Egyptian Museum.
king.quote: A Greek king in pharaoh's clothing — and a city the sea tried to keep.
king.cite: — On the Ptolemaic colossi of Thonis-Heracleion

column.pageTitle: Column of King Merenptah — Grand Egyptian Museum
column.subtitle: A pharaoh's war record carved in red granite — "writing for eternity"
column.lead: Carved from red granite on a limestone base, this 5.6-metre column commemorates the military victories of King Merenptah — the thirteenth son and eventual successor of Ramses II — and the moment the name "Israel" first appears in world history.
column.body1: By the time he took the throne in 1213 BC, Merenptah was already elderly, having outlived most of his brothers during his father's 66-year reign. His own decade of rule was short but turbulent: Egypt's western frontier was under attack from Libyan tribes allied with the mysterious Sea Peoples, and unrest was spreading across the Levant. This column records his victories over them.
column.body2: Four sides of the shaft are covered in scenes of royal power. Merenptah is shown receiving a mace from the warrior goddess Anath, and the sword of victory from Amun, Re-Horakhty, Seth, Montu, and the lioness goddess Sekhmet. Bearded captives are bound with ropes just above the base, and the inscription records the spoils of battle — gold, silver, horses, goats, bows and arrows — taken from those who had "transgressed the borders of Egypt."
column.body3: The column originally stood at a temple Merenptah built at Heliopolis — the ancient City of the Sun, today buried beneath the Cairo suburb of Matariya. Discovered in 1970, it was taken to Cairo's Salaheddin Citadel in 2008 for a decade of conservation, as rising groundwater was threatening the surrounding area. In 2018 it was transported to its permanent home in the atrium of the Grand Egyptian Museum, where it stands today among the colossi of Ramses II and the Ptolemaic kings.
column.quote: Take your mace so that you may kill the rebels.

hatshepsut.pageTitle: Hatshepsut — The Female Pharaoh | Grand Egyptian Museum
hatshepsut.subtitle: The woman who ruled as king — Egypt's most powerful female pharaoh
hatshepsut.lead: Hatshepsut was one of ancient Egypt's most successful pharaohs — a woman who declared herself king, wore a false beard, and ruled the most powerful empire on earth for more than twenty years.
hatshepsut.body1: Born around 1507 BC, Hatshepsut was the daughter of Thutmose I and queen consort to Thutmose II. When her husband died, she became regent for her young stepson Thutmose III — but within a few years she had claimed the full titles and regalia of pharaoh, presenting herself in sculpture and relief wearing the double crown, royal kilt, and braided false beard that marked the divine kingship.
hatshepsut.body2: Her reign was defined not by war but by trade and building. She sent a celebrated expedition to the land of Punt, returning with myrrh trees, ivory, ebony, and exotic animals. She erected two towering obelisks at Karnak — the tallest in the world at the time — and built her magnificent mortuary temple at Deir el-Bahari, regarded as one of the greatest architectural achievements of ancient Egypt.
hatshepsut.body3: After her death, Thutmose III ordered many of her images erased from monuments and her statues smashed — an attempt to erase her from history. She was forgotten for centuries. Only in the 19th century did scholars decoding hieroglyphs reveal that she had been female, restoring Hatshepsut to her rightful place as one of Egypt's most extraordinary rulers.
hatshepsut.quote: I am the king's daughter. I am the king's wife. I am the king.

boat.pageTitle: Solar Boat of Khufu — Grand Egyptian Museum
boat.subtitle: The world's oldest intact ship — assembled without a single nail
boat.lead: Built around 2500 BC for the pharaoh Khufu — the builder of the Great Pyramid — the Solar Boat is the oldest intact ship in the world. A 43.4-metre vessel of Lebanon cedar, assembled without a single metal nail.
boat.body1: On May 26, 1954, Egyptian archaeologist Kamal el-Mallakh uncovered a sealed limestone pit on the south side of the Great Pyramid. Drilling a small hole through one of the massive blocks, he lowered a mirror and caught the first glimpse of 1,224 wooden pieces — carefully dismantled, methodically arranged, and preserved untouched for nearly 4,500 years. A faint scent of ancient cedar still rose from the chamber.
boat.body2: The reassembly fell to master restorer Ahmed Youssef Moustafa, who spent more than a decade studying ancient boat reliefs and traditional Nile shipwrights to understand how the vessel was built. The ship uses a shell-first construction method: planks of cedar joined by mortise-and-tenon joints of Christ's-thorn wood, then lashed together with ropes of halfa grass. It has been called a masterpiece of woodcraft — so well made that experts say it could sail today if placed in water.
boat.body3: Called a solar barque, the boat was built to carry the resurrected king across the sky with the sun god Ra, rising at dawn and descending through the underworld at night. Whether it ever touched the Nile is debated; some traces suggest it may have served as a funerary vessel to carry Khufu's body to Giza before being dismantled and buried. In August 2021, after forty years in a small museum beside the pyramid, the 20-ton ship was transferred on a remote-controlled shock-absorbing vehicle to its permanent home at the Grand Egyptian Museum.
boat.quote: A masterpiece of woodcraft — so well made it could sail today.
boat.cite: — On the Khufu ship, the world's oldest intact vessel

thutmose.pageTitle: Seated Statue of Thutmose III — Grand Egyptian Museum
thutmose.subtitle: The Napoleon of Ancient Egypt — enthroned in granite, his feet resting on the enemies of the world
thutmose.lead: This seated granite colossus captures Thutmose III at the height of his power — a king who built Egypt's largest empire and whose military genius earned him the title "Napoleon of Ancient Egypt" from modern historians.
thutmose.body1: The king sits on a royal throne with the composure of a man who has never lost a battle. His hands rest palms-down on his knees — a pose of controlled authority, neither aggressive nor passive. He wears the nemes headcloth and the shendyt royal kilt. Beneath his feet, carved into the base, are the Nine Bows — the traditional symbol of Egypt's enemies, permanently crushed under the weight of the throne.
thutmose.body2: Thutmose III came to power as a child, forced to share the throne with his stepmother Hatshepsut for 22 years while she held the full powers of pharaoh. After her death, he ruled alone — and made up for lost time. He led at least 17 military campaigns into Syria, Canaan, and Nubia. His decisive victory at the Battle of Megiddo, one of the earliest battles recorded in detailed historical accounts, became a masterclass in military strategy still studied today.
thutmose.body3: Discovered at Karnak in 1859, this statue stood at the very temple complex where Thutmose III inscribed his military annals on the walls — a monument to a king who carved his record into both stone and history.
thutmose.quote: He is like a flame when it goes forth, and there is none that can stand before him.
thutmose.cite: — From the Annals of Thutmose III, Karnak

obelisk.subtitle: The world's first suspended obelisk — revealing a cartouche hidden for 3,500 years
obelisk.lead: Standing 16 meters tall and weighing 87 tons, the Hanging Obelisk is the first artefact to greet visitors at the Grand Egyptian Museum — and the first obelisk in the world to be displayed suspended in mid-air.
obelisk.body1: Carved from a single block of red granite quarried at Aswan, the obelisk was originally raised by Ramses II at Tanis in the eastern Nile Delta, where the pharaoh erected more than ten such monuments. It is the tallest obelisk ever to have stood at that ancient city, and its four sides bear inscriptions proclaiming Ramses's Horus name, throne name, and birth name — along with epithets like "Beloved of Amun" and "Strong Bull."
obelisk.body2: Discovered broken in fragments during 20th-century excavations, the obelisk was transported to Giza for painstaking restoration. During that work, archaeologists noticed something extraordinary on the underside of its base: a rare cartouche of Ramses II, carved in a place that would normally be hidden forever once the obelisk was raised. The king had likely inscribed it there to safeguard his name from future rulers who might try to usurp his monuments by re-carving the visible faces.
obelisk.body3: That hidden cartouche inspired the museum's architects to do what had never been done before — elevate the obelisk on a four-column steel frame so that visitors could walk beneath it, look up through a glass floor, and read an inscription invisible for over three and a half thousand years. Advanced base isolators shield the 87-ton monument from the vibrations of nearby traffic and the Cairo Metro, making preservation and spectacle coexist.
obelisk.quote: An inscription hidden for 3,500 years, now visible above the heads of those who walk beneath it.

tut.subtitle: The Golden King · 1332 – 1323 BC
tut.hist1Body: Tutankhamun, often called King Tut, was an ancient Egyptian pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty. He became king at a very young age — likely around 8 or 9 years old — during the turbulent aftermath of his father Akhenaten's religious revolution.
tut.hist2Body: His reign was relatively short and not politically significant compared to other pharaohs. However, he is known for restoring traditional Egyptian religion after the controversial reign of his predecessor, Akhenaten, who had introduced the worship of a single god.
tut.hist3Body: Tutankhamun died at about 18 or 19 years old, and the exact cause of his death remains uncertain. Some studies suggest illness or injury, while others point to genetic conditions inherited from the close bloodlines of the royal family.
tut.hist4Body: What made Tutankhamun world-famous was the discovery of his nearly intact tomb by British archaeologist Howard Carter in the Valley of the Kings. The tomb, known as KV62, contained over 5,000 artifacts, including his iconic gold funerary mask.
tut.histOutro: Today, Tutankhamun is one of the most famous figures of ancient Egypt — a symbol of the richness, mystery, and artistry of the pharaonic civilization.
tut.healthBannerTitle: Want to know the medical secrets behind Tutankhamun's mummy?
tut.healthBannerSub: Explore his genetic conditions, injuries, and the diseases of his era — backed by real scientific research.

grandHall.pageTitle: The Grand Hall — Grand Egyptian Museum
grandHall.subtitle: Where 3,200 years of kingship welcome the modern visitor
grandHall.hist1Body: A single block of red granite was quarried at Aswan and carved into an 11-metre statue of Ramses II, the third pharaoh of the Nineteenth Dynasty. It was transported down the Nile and raised at the Temple of Ptah in the ancient capital of Memphis, where it would stand for more than three thousand years.
grandHall.hist2Body: Italian explorer Giovanni Battista Caviglia — the same archaeologist who excavated the Great Sphinx — found the statue lying on its side at Mit Rahina, broken into six fragments. Offered to an Italian duke and then to the British Museum, both declined as too costly to move. The colossus remained in the sand for another 135 years.
grandHall.hist3Body: Under Egyptian Prime Minister Gamal Abdel Nasser, the fragments were restored and raised outside Cairo's main railway station. For half a century the colossus stood as a silent sentinel in the heart of the capital — surrounded by traffic and slowly corroded by the city's pollution.
grandHall.hist4Body: After a ten-hour overnight journey through Cairo in 2006, and a decade of restoration at Giza, the colossus was placed in its permanent home — the atrium of the Grand Egyptian Museum. The building itself was designed around the statue, since it could not have fit through any completed door. Today it stands beneath a glass roof, illuminated at equinox by a beam of sunlight that echoes the ancient alignment at Abu Simbel.
grandHall.histOutro: Spanning 10,000 square metres beneath a translucent alabaster façade, the Grand Hall is the threshold between the Nile Valley and the Giza Plateau — a space where the visitor stands, for a moment, between the world of the kings and the world of today.

stairs.pageTitle: The Grand Staircase — Grand Egyptian Museum
stairs.subtitle: A vertical gallery of kings, gods, and eternity
stairs.hist1Body: The ascent begins with the face of kingship itself. Colossal seated and standing statues of pharaohs — from Senwosret III of the Middle Kingdom to Hatshepsut, Thutmose III, Amenhotep III, and even the Roman emperor Caracalla portrayed as pharaoh — show how each ruler chose to present himself to gods, subjects, and eternity.
stairs.hist2Body: Here the temples themselves come into focus. Obelisk tips, temple lintels, and architectural fragments — including a gilded tip from an obelisk of Hatshepsut — recall the great sanctuaries of Karnak, Luxor, and beyond, where the pharaoh built permanence into stone for the gods.
stairs.hist3Body: Midway up the staircase, the pharaoh meets the divine. Statues depict kings standing, kneeling, or embracing the gods they served — Sekhmet the lioness goddess of war, Ptah the creator, and Re-Horakhty the morning sun — illustrating the sacred bond between the ruler and the powers of the cosmos.
stairs.hist4Body: The final ascent is funerary. Stone sarcophagi from across three millennia and a lone pyramidion from an unknown 13th Dynasty king at Saqqara prepare the visitor for the last step — a panoramic window opening onto the Giza Pyramids, the eternal houses of the kings.
stairs.histOutro: Rising six stories through 6,000 square metres, the Grand Staircase is both architecture and narrative — a climb from the earliest kings to the splendour of the New Kingdom, ending with the pyramids themselves framed in glass.

tutHealth.heroSubEn: A medical journey into the life and death of Tutankhamun
tutHealth.heroSubAr: رحلة طبية في حياة وموت توت عنخ آمون

fam.title: A Dynasty Too Close
fam.sub: Centuries of inbreeding within the royal family caused a cascade of inherited conditions
fam.genesSub: Direct consequences of generational royal inbreeding
fam.gene1Desc: A congenital foot deformity twisting the foot inward. Forced the king to rely on walking sticks — over 130 were found in his tomb.
fam.gene2Desc: Bone death caused by disrupted blood supply to the metatarsals. Found in the second and third metatarsal bones of the left foot.
fam.gene3Desc: A split in the roof of the mouth present from birth. A known marker of congenital abnormality common in offspring of closely related parents.
fam.gene4Desc: Inbreeding reduces genetic diversity, compromising the immune response. Tutankhamun carried at least four strains of malaria — suggesting a severely weakened defence system.

dis.title: Diseases of the Pharaohs' Era
dis.sub: The ancient world was rife with infectious and parasitic illness — many confirmed in royal mummies
dis.d1desc: The deadliest form of malaria, spread by Anopheles mosquitoes thriving along the Nile. Four strains of P. falciparum DNA were found in Tutankhamun's remains — the oldest confirmed malaria infection in recorded history.
dis.d2desc: A parasitic disease caused by flatworms living in Nile water. Eggs found in several Egyptian mummies. The parasite caused chronic internal bleeding, organ damage, and severe fatigue — a silent killer that weakened the population for millennia.
dis.d3desc: Evidence of TB has been found in Egyptian mummies dating back to 3000 BC. The disease attacked the lungs and spine, leaving distinctive lesions on vertebrae. While not confirmed in Tutankhamun himself, TB was endemic throughout the Nile Valley during the New Kingdom.
dis.d4desc: Studies of ancient Egyptian remains have documented roundworm, tapeworm, and Guinea worm infections. Contaminated water and food made parasitic disease ubiquitous across all social classes — royalty included.
dis.d5desc: CT scans of 52 Egyptian mummies revealed calcified arteries consistent with heart disease — proving that atherosclerosis is not a modern condition. A high-salt, high-fat diet combined with genetic predisposition made it common among the elite.
dis.d6desc: Sand-contaminated bread ground on stone mills wore Egyptian teeth to the pulp. Almost every adult mummy shows severe dental attrition. Tooth abscesses were common causes of fatal infection.

tl.title: The Research Timeline
tl.sub: From Howard Carter's torch to modern DNA sequencing — a hundred years of uncovering the truth
tl.e1body: Howard Carter and Lord Carnarvon unseal the nearly intact tomb of Tutankhamun in the Valley of the Kings. Over 5,000 objects are catalogued over the next decade — including 130+ walking canes that would later hint at the king's mobility problems.
tl.e2body: Anatomist Douglas Derry performs the first full examination of the royal mummy. He estimates the king was 18–22 years old at death and notes bone immaturity. Skeletal abnormalities go largely unrecognised at this stage.
tl.e3body: A University of Liverpool team X-rays the mummy and finds loose bone fragments inside the skull, sparking decades of debate about a possible blow to the head. Later CT analysis would reveal these fragments likely shifted post-mummification.
tl.e4body: The Egyptian Supreme Council of Antiquities conducts a full CT scan of the mummy — 1,700 cross-sectional images. The team identifies a left leg fracture, avascular necrosis in multiple bones, and the clubfoot deformity. The murder hypothesis is largely dismissed.
tl.e5body: Zahi Hawass leads a landmark study published in JAMA. DNA from 11 royal mummies establishes a five-generation family tree. The study confirms: Tutankhamun's parents were siblings, he carried four malaria strains, and he had multiple bone diseases.
tl.e6body: A BBC documentary team uses 2,000 CT scans to build a virtual 3D model of Tutankhamun's body. The model confirms a severely deformed left foot and multiple inherited conditions. A new theory emerges: the king may have been struck by a chariot wheel.

ref.title: References
ref.sub: All findings on this page are drawn from peer-reviewed research and primary archaeological sources
ref.r1note: The landmark study that identified malaria, bone disease, and sibling parentage using DNA from 11 royal mummies. The primary source for most modern findings about Tutankhamun's health.
ref.r2note: 1,700 CT cross-sections produced from the 2005 Egyptian Mummy Project. Identified the clubfoot, bone necrosis, and the left leg fracture that likely occurred close to death.
ref.r3label: Virtual Autopsy — Ashraf Selim et al. (2014)
ref.r3note: The most detailed post-mortem reconstruction yet. Confirmed a scoliosis-like spinal curvature, the deformed left foot, and a complex chest fracture. Introduced the chariot accident hypothesis.
ref.r4note: The first systematic radiological study of the mummy. Identified loose bone fragments in the skull, fuelling the murder debate. Blood type O was determined — later disputed as unreliable from degraded samples.
ref.r5note: The first formal anatomical report on the mummy. Estimated age at death as 18–22 years. Limited by the tools of the time but remained the primary reference for decades.
ref.r6label: Atherosclerosis in Mummies — Allam et al. (2011)
ref.r6note: CT scanning of 52 ancient Egyptian mummies — evidence of atherosclerosis in 45% of subjects, proving cardiovascular disease predates modern lifestyle factors.

level1.subtitle: How pharaohs chose to be remembered

level2.subtitle: The temples of Egypt, remembered in stone

level3.subtitle: Where the pharaoh meets the divine

level4.pageTitle: Journey to Eternity — The Grand Staircase
level4.subtitle: Where the king becomes a star, and the stairs become a horizon

royalImage.intro: The ascent begins here, at the face of kingship itself. Every pharaoh commissioned statues to shape how he — or she — would be remembered: as a warrior, a god on earth, or a guardian of Ma'at. This level gathers the most striking portraits from three millennia of royal self-presentation.
royalImage.hist1Body: Royal portraiture in the Middle Kingdom broke from tradition. Where Old Kingdom pharaohs had been shown as ageless, divine, and serene, Senwosret III was carved with heavy-lidded eyes, furrowed brow, and downturned mouth — the face of a ruler who felt the weight of kingship. It was one of the first honest portraits in history.
royalImage.hist2Body: Under the Thutmosides and Amenhotep III, royal sculpture reached a peak of refined idealism. Hatshepsut, ruling as pharaoh, had herself depicted with the traditional false beard and royal kilt — a woman performing the role of king in stone. After her death, her nephew Thutmose III defaced her monuments and claimed many for himself.
royalImage.hist3Body: Then came the strangest king in Egypt's history. Akhenaten abandoned the old gods, moved the capital, and commissioned statues of himself with an elongated skull, narrow slanting eyes, a long face, and swollen hips. Whether stylized or medically literal, these images shattered a thousand years of idealized royal portraiture.
royalImage.hist4Body: The Ramessides returned to tradition — monumental, triumphal, warrior-kings carved at vast scale. A millennium later, the Roman emperor Caracalla dressed himself in pharaonic regalia for propaganda: a Roman conqueror claiming the old gods' sanction. The staircase closes the circle — from Senwosret's honest face to Caracalla's borrowed crown.
royalImage.histOutro: Every pharaoh, from the weary Middle Kingdom realist to the Roman emperor in Egyptian clothes, made the same decision: how would stone remember them? The answers line this level of the staircase.
